content.text: Browser JavaScript running on one origin cannot freely read HTTP responses from a different origin — this is the same-origin policy enforced by every modern browser. CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is the standardised mechanism that lets servers selectively relax that restriction by sending declarative HTTP headers. Understanding CORS — along with HTTPS, CSRF protection, secret handling, and input validation — is essential for anyone building agentic AI systems that expose APIs to browser clients or that integrate with third-party services.
is the triple of scheme, host, and port derived from a URL. The URL
(port 443 is implicit for HTTPS). Two URLs share an origin only when all three components match exactly — different ports or subdomains are cross-origin.

is a browser security boundary: a script loaded from origin A can send requests to origin B, but the browser withholds the response from the script unless origin B explicitly permits the read. This prevents a malicious page from silently exfiltrating data from a banking API that the user is authenticated against.
is the W3C-standardised escape hatch. A server opt-in via response headers — principally
— tells the browser which foreign origins may read the response. For requests that could have side effects (non-idempotent methods, custom headers), the browser first sends a preflight
request to confirm the server accepts the call before transmitting the real request.

extend beyond CORS to include transport security (HTTPS), authentication tokens, CSRF protection, secret management, and input validation. CORS is one layer in a defence-in-depth stack — not a substitute for authentication.

Modern agentic AI systems span multiple layers: a browser-based chat UI, a backend agent API, and one or more third-party LLM endpoints. Every boundary between these layers introduces a potential security failure:
is subject to CORS. If the agent API runs on a different domain than the chat UI, the browser blocks responses unless the server sends the correct
is a server-to-server call. CORS does not apply — but API key security, TLS pinning, and egress allow-listing do.
on the agent endpoint prevents unauthenticated callers from triggering expensive LLM invocations or accessing other users' data.
errors — embedding LLM API keys in browser JavaScript bundles — are among the most common and costly mistakes in AI product development.

Misconfigured CORS breaks UIs in production (a common shipping incident). Overly permissive CORS or missing authentication exposes user data and billing accounts to abuse. Developers who understand these layers ship faster and build more trustworthy products.
— the same-origin policy prevents any arbitrary website from reading API responses on behalf of a logged-in user.
— CORS origin allowlists make the set of trusted frontend origins a visible, auditable configuration decision.
— for requests with side effects the browser confirms intent before sending data, blocking cross-origin state mutations from malicious pages.
— combining CORS, HTTPS, auth, CSRF tokens, and rate limits means no single misconfiguration creates a catastrophic breach.
— the BFF (backend-for-frontend) pattern that CORS encourages also keeps LLM API keys off the client, improving key rotation and audit logging.
— curl, Python httpx, Postman, and server-side agents are not bound by CORS. Treating CORS as an access control mechanism for non-browser callers creates a false sense of security.
— every non-simple cross-origin request incurs an extra round-trip for the OPTIONS preflight. High-frequency agent polling APIs should cache preflight results using
— when cookies or HTTP auth are included (
), the server must set a specific origin (not
. Mixing wildcard with credentials is invalid and confusing to debug.
— naively echoing the incoming Origin header into ACAO without validation effectively disables CORS protection and opens the API to every website.
— local dev often uses a Vite/webpack proxy that hides CORS, so the app works locally but fails when deployed to a separate domain.
to a cross-origin URL, the browser attaches an
header to the request. After the server responds, the browser inspects the response headers before handing the body to JavaScript. If
), the browser exposes the response. Otherwise it discards it and raises a CORS error — the request still reached the server and was processed.
CORS errors happen on the response, not the request
. The server has already received and executed the call. CORS only controls whether the browser lets JavaScript read the response. This is why non-browser attackers are not stopped by CORS.
(no preflight) when it meets all of: method is GET, HEAD, or POST; headers are restricted to
with value application/x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, or text/plain. Any deviation — including

request the browser sends before the real request. The server must respond with appropriate access-control headers; only then does the browser send the actual call. The preflight result can be cached for up to 86 400 seconds to avoid per-request overhead.

# Preflight request the browser sends automatically
OPTIONS /api/chat HTTP/1.1
Host: api.myagent.com
Origin: https://chat.example.com
Access-Control-Request-Method: POST
Access-Control-Request-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization

# Server preflight response (must include these headers)
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://chat.example.com
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, OPTIONS
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) is distinct from CORS. A malicious site can craft an HTML form that submits a POST to your agent API from the victim's browser, and cookies are sent automatically — CORS does not block this because the attacker does not need to read the response, only trigger the side effect.
Mitigations: synchroniser (CSRF) tokens embedded in forms; the

cookie attribute (prevents cross-site cookie sending); double-submit cookie pattern; or switching to token-based auth (Bearer tokens in the Authorization header are not sent automatically by browsers, so CSRF is not an issue).

CORS headers and authentication tokens are meaningless over plain HTTP, where a network attacker can intercept and modify headers. All agent APIs and browser frontends must use TLS 1.2 or 1.3. Configure
(HSTS) to prevent downgrade attacks, and redirect HTTP to HTTPS at the ingress layer.

The diagram below shows the full request lifecycle for a browser-based agent chat UI. The dashed yellow arrows represent the preflight exchange; solid blue is the actual data flow; the red dashed line shows a cross-origin request from a malicious site that the browser blocks because the origin is not in the allowlist.

attrs.alt: CORS and web security architecture: browser, agent API, LLM API, malicious site blocked

content.text: Key observations: (1) the LLM API call is entirely server-to-server — no CORS headers are required there; (2) the API key stays on the agent server and is never sent to the browser; (3) the agent API applies multiple security checks before invoking the LLM; (4) CSRF and HTTPS are orthogonal concerns applied at separate layers.
Building an agent API (FastAPI, Express, Django) that a browser-based chat UI or embedded widget calls from a different domain.
Serving a public API that third-party web apps are permitted to call from their own origins.
Exposing a streaming SSE or WebSocket endpoint consumed by browser clients.
Your Python agent calls LLM APIs directly — server-to-server HTTP bypasses all browser enforcement.
The frontend and backend share exactly the same origin (same scheme, host, port) — the same-origin policy allows reads automatically.
You are building a CLI tool or notebook-only workflow with no browser UI.
Do not use CORS as the sole security gate
CORS does not stop curl, Postman, Python requests, or any non-browser client.
Always authenticate requests independently; CORS is a browser-side origin read restriction, not an API firewall.
Step 1 — set up the BFF pattern

is the agent API that sits between the browser and the LLM. The browser authenticates with the BFF (JWT or session cookie); the BFF holds the LLM API key and forwards requests on the user's behalf. This pattern solves CORS (one controlled domain), key security (never client-side), and rate limiting (centralised per-user quota).

# requirements: fastapi uvicorn python-jose[cryptography] httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException, status
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.security import HTTPBearer, HTTPAuthorizationCredentials
from jose import jwt, JWTError
import httpx, os

app = FastAPI()

ALLOWED_ORIGINS = os.environ["ALLOWED_ORIGINS"].split(",")  # e.g. "https://chat.example.com"
JWT_SECRET = os.environ["JWT_SECRET"]
LLM_API_KEY = os.environ["LLM_API_KEY"]  # never sent to browser

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=ALLOWED_ORIGINS,      # explicit allowlist — never use ["*"] with auth
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["GET", "POST", "OPTIONS"],
    allow_headers=["Content-Type", "Authorization"],
    max_age=86400,                       # cache preflight 24 h
)

bearer = HTTPBearer()

def verify_token(credentials: HTTPAuthorizationCredentials = Depends(bearer)):
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(credentials.credentials, JWT_SECRET, algorithms=["HS256"])
        return payload["sub"]
    except JWTError:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="Invalid token")


@app.post("/api/chat")
async def chat(body: dict, user_id: str = Depends(verify_token)):
    # LLM_API_KEY stays server-side — browser never sees it
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.post(
            "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages",
            headers={"x-api-key": LLM_API_KEY, "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01"},
            json={
                "model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
                "max_tokens": 1024,
                "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": body["message"]}],
            },
            timeout=30,
        )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()
After deploying, verify CORS is correctly configured by inspecting the preflight response in browser DevTools (Network tab → filter 'OPTIONS') and with curl:

# Simulate preflight
curl -i -X OPTIONS https://api.myagent.com/api/chat \
  -H "Origin: https://chat.example.com" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization"

# Expected response headers:
# Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://chat.example.com
# Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, OPTIONS
# Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
# Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400
Instead of hard-coding a single string, maintain an allowlist and validate dynamically. This supports multi-tenant deployments where each customer has their own frontend domain.
Example 2 — CSRF token pattern for cookie-based auth (JavaScript + Python)

When using cookies for session auth instead of Bearer tokens, CSRF tokens are required. The server issues a CSRF token in the response body (or a separate header) and the browser echoes it as a custom header on state-mutating calls.
Consider an AI coding assistant embedded in a SaaS IDE. The product has three components: a React frontend served from
The React app authenticates users with JWTs (issued by the auth service on login). All chat requests POST to
with the JWT as a Bearer token and
— both conditions trigger a CORS preflight. The agent API sets
exclusively; no wildcard is used, because the API returns user-specific code completions.

The Anthropic API key is stored in AWS Secrets Manager and injected into the agent container at startup as an environment variable. It is never logged, never returned in responses, and rotated quarterly. The agent API calls Anthropic server-to-server — Anthropic sees no CORS headers at all.

Rate limiting is applied per user_id at the agent API: 100 requests/minute, with HTTP 429 returned on excess. Each agent run is logged with the user_id, prompt hash length, and latency — but not prompt content, for privacy. An automatic alert fires if any single user_id exceeds 500 requests in 5 minutes (possible key theft).

# WRONG: browsers reject Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * when credentials: 'include'
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["*"],     # invalid with allow_credentials=True
    allow_credentials=True,  # browsers block and raise CORS error
)

# CORRECT: list specific trusted origins
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["https://chat.example.com"],
    allow_credentials=True,
)

# WRONG: reflects any Origin header — effectively disables CORS protection
@app.middleware("http")
async def bad_cors(request, call_next):
    response = await call_next(request)
    response.headers["Access-Control-Allow-Origin"] = request.headers.get("origin", "*")
    return response

# CORRECT: validate against an allowlist before reflecting
ALLOWED = {"https://chat.example.com", "https://admin.example.com"}

@app.middleware("http")
async def good_cors(request, call_next):
    origin = request.headers.get("origin", "")
    response = await call_next(request)
    if origin in ALLOWED:
        response.headers["Access-Control-Allow-Origin"] = origin
        response.headers["Vary"] = "Origin"
    return response
Mistake 3 — LLM API key in the browser bundle
Mistake 4 — assuming CORS blocks non-browser clients

CORS is enforced exclusively by the browser's JavaScript engine. A curl command, Python requests, Postman, or any server-side agent can call your API without CORS headers and will receive the full response. If an endpoint should be restricted, implement authentication and network-level controls.
to the specific origins you trust. Wildcards are acceptable only for fully public, unauthenticated, read-only endpoints (e.g. a public CDN for static assets).
— whenever you reflect the incoming Origin header, add
to the response so CDNs and reverse proxies do not serve a cached response for origin A to a request from origin B.
to avoid redundant OPTIONS round-trips for active users.
Proxy LLM calls through the backend
— the BFF pattern keeps all LLM API keys server-side and centralises audit logging, rate limiting, and prompt sanitisation.
on session cookies as a first line of CSRF defence; add CSRF tokens for full protection on state-mutating endpoints.
— redirect HTTP to HTTPS at the load balancer; set HSTS with a long max-age; use
Store secrets in environment variables or secret managers
— never commit API keys to git. Use AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or platform-native secret injection.
— agent endpoints that execute tool calls should validate types, lengths, and formats before acting on user-supplied data.
CORS middleware is computationally trivial — an origin check against a small set costs microseconds. The main performance concern is preflight latency.
to 86 400 (24 h, the browser maximum) to eliminate repeated OPTIONS round-trips. Chrome enforces a maximum of 7 200 s; Firefox accepts 86 400 s.
— for polling endpoints that use GET with no custom headers, design the API to avoid triggering preflights entirely.
— if a CORS-enabled endpoint is also cacheable (e.g. public data), include
to ensure the CDN caches separate copies per origin.
— enforce rate limits at the gateway or reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, Kong), not inside the application, to fail fast before reaching the LLM call and consuming tokens.
— the agent API should maintain a persistent httpx or aiohttp connection pool to the LLM endpoint rather than opening a new TCP connection per request.

# FastAPI's built-in CORSMiddleware — wraps Starlette's implementation
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["https://chat.example.com"],
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
    allow_credentials=True,
    max_age=86400,
)

# pip install django-cors-headers
INSTALLED_APPS = ["corsheaders", ...]
MIDDLEWARE = ["corsheaders.middleware.CorsMiddleware", ...]

CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS = ["https://chat.example.com"]
CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS = True
— Network tab, filter by 'Options' to inspect preflight requests and response headers. Console shows CORS error messages.
— a proxy for bypassing CORS in development (never use in production).
— security scanners that test for CORS misconfiguration, including origin reflection and wildcard credential issues.
Authenticated agent APIs with browser clients
Traditional web apps with form-based login

Agent systems that incorporate user-supplied URLs, file names, or free-text into tool calls are vulnerable to prompt injection. A user could supply a URL that points to a document containing instructions intended to hijack the agent's behaviour. Mitigate by validating that URLs are on an allow-listed domain before fetching, and by treating fetched content as untrusted data rather than instructions.
If an agent tool accepts arbitrary URLs and fetches them server-side (e.g. a 'fetch web page' tool), an attacker may supply internal network addresses (
for Redis) to enumerate or attack internal infrastructure. Block private IP ranges and use egress allow-listing.

LLM API keys should be rotated immediately on suspicion of compromise. Use separate keys per environment (dev/staging/prod) and per deployment so a leaked dev key does not affect production. Apply spend limits on LLM provider dashboards as a circuit breaker against runaway agent loops or key theft.
— set aggressive HTTP timeouts on LLM calls (30–120 s depending on task) and implement exponential backoff with jitter on 429 (rate limited) and 5xx responses.
— if the LLM API fails repeatedly, open the circuit and return a graceful degraded response rather than queuing requests.
— agent endpoints that trigger expensive side effects should accept an idempotency key so clients can safely retry without double-executing.
CORS is a browser enforcement mechanism — it controls whether JavaScript can read a cross-origin response. It does not protect server-to-server calls or stop curl, Postman, or Python agent backends.
Always allowlist specific trusted origins rather than using
. Reflecting the incoming Origin header without validation disables protection entirely.

The BFF (backend-for-frontend) pattern is the correct architecture: browser authenticates with the agent API, agent API calls the LLM with a server-side key — the LLM API key never reaches the browser.
CSRF and CORS address different threats. Use SameSite cookies and CSRF tokens for cookie-authenticated endpoints; Bearer tokens in the Authorization header are inherently CSRF-safe.
to avoid per-request OPTIONS round-trips in active browser sessions.
Validate URLs before agent tool calls to prevent SSRF; treat fetched external content as untrusted data to prevent prompt injection.

Layer defences: HTTPS everywhere, explicit CORS allowlists, authentication on every endpoint, rate limiting per user, secret rotation, and input validation together form a robust security posture for agentic AI systems.
